title: Spectral Gap of the Laplacian for Random Hyperbolic Surfaces - Part 2
speaker: Nalini Anantharaman
abstract: Although there are several ways to ''choose a compact hyperbolic surface at random'', putting the Weil-Petersson probability measure on the moduli space of hyperbolic surfaces of a given topology is certainly the most natural. The work of M. Mirzakhani has made possible the study of this probabilistic model, providing exact formulas for certain integrals, as well as their asymptotic behaviour in the limit of large genus.

proof_stages.label: Local topological types and volume functions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines a local topological type as a filling closed curve in a fixed bordered surface, then introduces the associated moduli-space integral and its density/volume function, whose normalized large-genus expansion starts at minus the Euler characteristic of the small surface.

proof_stages.label: Friedman-type admissible functions and convolution closure
proof_stages.summary: A target class of one-variable functions is defined by an asymptotic polynomial-times-exponential form with a lower-order remainder, and the key formal property is that this class is stable under convolution, so polynomial leading pieces will later be killed by integration by parts without computing their coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Convolution paradigm from the graph case
proof_stages.summary: The graph analogue explains why convolution should appear: after decomposing a curve/walk into simple pieces, its graph length is a sum of piece lengths, whereas in the hyperbolic surface case the geodesic length is only approximately additive.

proof_stages.label: Integral formula using inside Teichmüller space and outside volumes
proof_stages.summary: The volume function is written as an integral over boundary lengths and hyperbolic metrics on the fixed small surface, multiplied by a Mirzakhani volume polynomial for the complementary surface; known recursion/asymptotic results give special-form coefficients and a leading term proportional to the product of sinh(x_i/2).

proof_stages.label: Reduction to fixed-dimensional coefficient integrals
proof_stages.summary: Substituting the large-genus asymptotic expansion of the outside volume into the integral reduces each coefficient f_k^T(l) to a fixed-dimensional integral over the slice where the chosen curve has hyperbolic length l, with no remaining genus parameter.

proof_stages.label: Length-comparison estimates and shielding reductions
proof_stages.summary: Elementary geodesic-minimization inequalities bound the sum of boundary lengths by twice the curve length, and refined notions of shielded and doubly filling simple portions improve this bound, showing that the hardest cases are generalized figure-eight curves with no shielded portions.

proof_stages.label: Opened-intersection coordinates and pseudo-convolution
proof_stages.summary: By opening self-intersections and using coordinates given by simple-curve lengths, angles, and orthogeodesic lengths, the relevant Weil-Petersson measure factor becomes an explicit product of admissible functions, while the curve length is an exactly controlled perturbation of a linear sum, yielding a pseudo-convolution argument for admissibility of the coefficients.

examples.name: Reidemeister-triangle move for topological type
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that flipping or moving triangular regions should not change the local topological type because the curve is remembered up to homotopy under a diffeomorphism.
examples.engagement_reason: the triangle/Reidemeister move is invoked only to explain the equivalence relation on topological types, with no computation or construction carried out on it

examples.name: Friedman graph length-sum model
examples.note: The graph case is presented as the source of the convolution idea: decomposing a closed walk into pieces makes its length an exact sum, so the corresponding contribution is a convolution of admissible functions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker concretely decomposes a Friedman graph curve into simple pieces, assigns lengths, uses the length-sum property, and derives the convolution form of the volume function

passes.reason: Friedman’s graph length-sum setting is a systematic ordinary graph model used to motivate convolution, not to disprove a claim.
